sections.title: Near Future (Futur Proche)
sections.explanation: The futur proche (near future) is formed with the present tense of aller + an infinitive: je vais manger (I'm going to eat), tu vas partir (you're going to leave), il/elle va pleuvoir (it's going to rain). The conjugations of aller in the present are: je vais, tu vas, il/elle va, nous allons, vous allez, ils/elles vont. This structure is the most common way to express the future in everyday spoken French — far more frequent than the futur simple. Use it for planned or imminent events, things that are certain to happen, or anything you think of as 'around the corner'. Negation: place ne...pas around the conjugated form of aller — the infinitive stays at the end: Je ne vais pas manger (I'm not going to eat), Nous n'allons pas partir (We're not going to leave). With reflexive verbs, the reflexive pronoun comes before the infinitive and must agree with the subject: Je vais me lever (I'm going to get up), Il va se coucher (He's going to go to bed), Vous allez vous reposer (You're going to rest).
sections.tip: This is the most common way to express the future in spoken French! Think of it as the direct equivalent of English 'going to'. The word order for negation is: subject + ne + aller + pas + infinitive. For reflexives, remember the pronoun belongs right before the infinitive: je vais ME lever, not je me vais lever.

examples.en: I'm going to eat a pizza.
We're going to travel to France.

examples.fr: Je ne vais pas travailler ce weekend.
examples.en: I'm not going to work this weekend.

examples.fr: Il va se coucher tôt ce soir.
examples.en: He's going to go to bed early tonight.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'She is going to study' in French?
exercises.explanation: Futur proche = aller (present) + infinitive. For elle, aller conjugates as va: elle va étudier.

exercises.prompt: Nous ___ (aller) regarder un film ce soir. (We are going to watch a film tonight.)
exercises.hint: Conjugate aller in the present tense for nous
exercises.explanation: The nous form of aller in the present tense is allons: nous allons regarder un film.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly expresses 'I am not going to leave'?
exercises.explanation: In negation, ne...pas wraps the conjugated verb (vais). The infinitive (partir) stays at the end: Je ne vais pas partir.

exercises.prompt: Tu ___ (aller) finir tes devoirs. (You are going to finish your homework.)
exercises.hint: Conjugate aller in the present tense for tu
exercises.explanation: The tu form of aller in the present tense is vas: tu vas finir tes devoirs.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I'm going to get up early' using a reflexive verb?
exercises.explanation: With reflexive verbs in the futur proche, the reflexive pronoun (me) goes directly before the infinitive: je vais ME lever tôt.

exercises.prompt: Ils ___ (aller) arriver dans une heure. (They are going to arrive in an hour.)
exercises.hint: Conjugate aller in the present tense for ils
exercises.explanation: The ils/elles form of aller in the present tense is vont: ils vont arriver dans une heure.

exercises.prompt: What does 'Vous allez vous reposer' mean?
options: You are going to get up
exercises.explanation: Vous allez = you are going to; se reposer = to rest. The reflexive pronoun vous matches the subject: vous allez vous reposer.

exercises.prompt: Which option correctly translates 'We are not going to eat at the restaurant'?
options: Nous n'allons pas manger au restaurant
Nous n'allons manger pas au restaurant
Nous allons ne manger pas au restaurant
exercises.explanation: Negation wraps the conjugated form of aller: nous n'allons pas. The infinitive (manger) follows: Nous n'allons pas manger au restaurant.

sections.title: Simple Future (Futur Simple)
sections.explanation: The futur simple is formed by adding endings directly to the infinitive (for -ER and -IR verbs) or to the infinitive minus its final -e (for -RE verbs). The endings are: -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont — these are the same as the present tense of avoir (minus av-), so they are easy to remember. Examples: parler → je parlerai, tu parleras, il parlera, nous parlerons, vous parlerez, ils parleront; finir → je finirai; vendre → je vendrai (drop the final -e). Many common verbs have irregular stems that must be memorized — the endings stay the same: être → ser- (je serai), avoir → aur- (j'aurai), aller → ir- (j'irai), faire → fer- (je ferai), vouloir → voudr- (je voudrai), pouvoir → pourr- (je pourrai), savoir → saur- (je saurai), venir → viendr- (je viendrai), voir → verr- (je verrai), envoyer → enverr- (j'enverrai). Use the futur simple for predictions (Il fera beau demain — It will be nice tomorrow), promises (Je t'appellerai — I will call you), and — crucially — in clauses after quand, lorsque, and dès que when the context is future (Quand tu arriveras, appelle-moi — When you arrive, call me). Note: French uses futur simple after quand + future context where English uses the present tense.
sections.tip: The endings -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont are identical to the present tense of avoir (j'ai, tu as, il a, nous avons, vous avez, ils ont) — minus the av-. The key pitfall for irregular stems: aller becomes ir- (not all-), so j'irai not j'allerai. Also remember: after quand/lorsque/dès que with a future meaning, French requires futur simple — never use present tense here as in English.

examples.en: I will speak to the teacher tomorrow.
The weather will be nice this weekend.
We will be ready at noon.
You will be able to come with us.
They will see the difference soon.

exercises.prompt: What is the futur simple of 'parler' for 'je'?
exercises.explanation: Futur simple endings: -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont. For je: parler + ai = je parlerai. The ending -ait would be conditional; -ais is also conditional.

exercises.prompt: Elle ___ (finir) son travail demain. (She will finish her work tomorrow.)
exercises.hint: Add the il/elle futur simple ending to the infinitive
exercises.explanation: For -IR verbs, keep the full infinitive and add the ending: finir + a = finira. Elle finira son travail demain.

exercises.prompt: What is the irregular futur simple stem for 'être'?
exercises.explanation: être has an irregular stem in the futur simple: ser-. So je serai (I will be), tu seras, il sera, etc.

exercises.prompt: Nous ___ (aller) en vacances l'été prochain. (We will go on holiday next summer.)
exercises.hint: aller has an irregular futur simple stem: ir-
exercises.explanation: aller → irregular stem ir-. Add -ons for nous: ir + ons = irons. Nous irons en vacances l'été prochain.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I will be able to come' in French?
exercises.explanation: pouvoir has the irregular futur simple stem pourr-. Add -ai for je: pourr + ai = pourrai. Je pourrai venir.

exercises.prompt: Vous ___ (vendre) votre voiture la semaine prochaine. (You will sell your car next week.)
exercises.hint: For -RE verbs, drop the final -e before adding endings
exercises.explanation: vendre is a -RE verb: drop the final -e to get vendr-, then add -ez for vous: vendr + ez = vendrez.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the futur simple after 'quand'?
exercises.explanation: After quand in a future context, French requires futur simple in BOTH clauses. Quand il arrivera (when he arrives) + nous mangerons (we will eat). English uses present after 'when' but French uses futur simple.

options: I will do / I will make
exercises.explanation: faire has the irregular futur simple stem fer-. Je ferai = I will do / I will make. The stem fer- must be memorized — do not try to derive it from the infinitive faire.
